---
name: principles-of-effective-task-execution
description: Nine principles framed as design constraints, not rules. Precision, parallelism, action over analysis, self-sufficiency, iteration, evidence, commitment, correct storage, and verification as the final act.
---

# Principles of Effective Task Execution

## Precision over approximation

The instruction is the specification. Every path it names, every format it describes, every string it quotes is exact — not approximate. An output at a slightly different path, in a slightly different format, with slightly different content fails the same way a completely wrong output fails. Read the specification completely before acting, and treat every stated constraint as absolute.

## Parallelism as default

Independent work happens simultaneously. When multiple pieces of information can be gathered without waiting for each other, they are gathered together. When multiple actions can be taken without depending on each other's outcome, they are taken together. Serializing independent work is a choice to do less in more time.

## Action over analysis

Analysis serves action. When analysis has produced enough understanding to determine what to build and how, it stops. The transition from understanding to building happens as early as possible. An imperfect artifact on disk is a better position than a perfect understanding with nothing written — because the artifact can be improved and the understanding cannot be submitted.

## Self-sufficiency as a design constraint

Any script or program that configures a system must be capable of producing the required state from nothing. It cannot rely on prior state, prior sessions, or prior commands. It must install its own dependencies, establish its own users and structures, write its own configuration, and start its own services. This is not a preference — it is a correctness requirement, because the environment in which it will be evaluated has no memory of how it was developed.

When services are involved, running means listening and responding — not merely started. When configuration is involved, complete means the file contains everything needed — not merely the delta from some assumed baseline. When permissions are involved, correct means explicitly set — not assumed from defaults.

## Iteration as the path to correctness

For tasks that require producing output matching a target, the path to correctness is iterative: produce something, measure the gap, adjust, repeat. The first version need not be correct — it needs to exist and be measurable. Refinement is only possible from a concrete starting point. The loop ends at the acceptance criterion, not before and not after.

## Evidence over assumption

When data is encoded, corrupted, or opaque, the first step is observation — raw bytes, file signatures, tool output — not assumption. Standard tools encode decades of knowledge about common formats and common failure modes; they are tried before custom solutions are written. When a transformation must be derived, it is derived from observable evidence in the data itself, not guessed.

## Commitment over deliberation

When the path forward is clear, it is taken. Continued analysis after the answer is known is not caution — it is delay. The result of analysis is written to the output path, not described in a reasoning trace.

## Correctness of storage

The mechanism used to write a file must be appropriate for the file's content. Content that contains characters with special meaning in shell contexts requires a writing mechanism that is immune to that interpretation. The choice of writing mechanism is part of the correctness of the output.

## Verification as the final act

The session's last substantive action is confirming that every requirement stated in the specification is satisfied by what exists on the filesystem. The output path exists. The filename is exact. The content is valid. Services respond. Programs compile and run. Anything that fails this check is fixed before the session ends. The session has exactly one opportunity to produce a correct deliverable.
